items.q: Why are top predators most affected by persistent pollutants like mercury?
items.correct: Biomagnification concentrates pollutants at each trophic level up the food chain.
items.misconception: Predators are exposed to more pollution directly.
items.why: Students focus on direct exposure. The mechanism is concentration through eating contaminated prey - small accumulations in lower levels multiply upward, so apex predators have the highest body burdens.

items.correct: The maximum population an environment can sustainably support given available resources.
items.misconception: How much an animal can physically carry.
items.random_wrong: The size a species can reach.
items.why: Students take 'carrying' literally. It's an ECOLOGICAL concept - the ceiling set by food, water, space, etc. Populations may temporarily exceed it but then crash, or oscillate around it.

items.q: Why are invasive species often more harmful than native species?
items.correct: They lack natural predators or competitors in their new environment, so they spread unchecked.
items.misconception: They are inherently more aggressive than native species.
items.random_wrong: They are larger than native species.
items.why: Students attribute success to species traits. The key factor is the ABSENCE OF CONTROLS - the same species may be ordinary in its native range, where co-evolved predators and competitors keep it in check.
Correct answer: Soil. “Water” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Vertical farms save roughly what percent of water vs field farming?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 95%. “10%” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Vertical leafy green yields per m2 can be roughly how many times field yields?
items.why: Exact answer: 10. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
